title: Executive Order 11375—Amending Executive Order No. 11246, Relating to Equal Employment Opportunity

flags.justification: This amends an existing framework (EO 11246) by expanding a protected category; it does not create new concentrated authority beyond the Secretary of Labor's already-existing compliance-review role.

The amended Sec. 104 requires the Civil Service Commission to provide 'prompt, fair, and impartial consideration of all complaints,' with 'at least one impartial review within the executive department or agency' and appeal to the Civil Service Commission — a genuine, textually explicit, multi-level complaint process.
Single coherent purpose: extending an existing equal-employment framework to cover sex discrimination.

The amended contractor obligations (Sec. 202) list concrete covered employment actions (upgrading, demotion, transfer, recruitment, layoff, pay, training selection) under an already-established Secretary of Labor compliance-review apparatus.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Permanence carve-out applies: a non-discrimination policy is evergreen by nature, consistent with EO 8802, EO 9981, and EO 11141.
This adds the explicit Civil Service Commission appeal mechanism (Sec. 104); no existing oversight is removed.

The amended Sec. 203(d) requires labor unions and referral agencies to certify their own non-discriminatory practices, with the Compliance Report required to document any refusal — a genuine, if imperfect, third-party accountability mechanism, alongside the Civil Service Commission appeal process.

This order's own text substantially expands equal-employment-opportunity coverage to sex discrimination across the entire federal contracting workforce — a major, textually explicit expansion of coverage with significant downstream legal and social implications, not merely a matter of later historical importance.
This amends the existing EO 11246 framework's own scope rather than redirecting another agency's mandate.
No new exemptions are created; broadening protected categories narrows rather than expands the space for permissible discrimination.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/20 = 5.0%. One of the most historically significant orders in this corpus — the extension of federal equal-employment protections to sex discrimination — yet one of the lowest-scoring, because it is a well-designed amendment to an already well-structured enforcement framework (multi-level complaint process, third-party certification requirement) rather than a novel, poorly-checked grant of power. Likely the cleanest illustration yet of the project's core design principle that structural weight and historical significance are distinct measures.
